attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a gradual onset of worsening shortness of breath over the past few days, associated with increased sputum production and a productive cough. His medical history includes a long-standing diagnosis of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and a significant smoking history. On examination, an increased use of accessory respiratory muscles and pursed-lip breathing is observed. Auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds and widespread wheezing throughout the lung fields.

Arterial blood gas analysis was performed:

  • pH: 7.30 (7.35-7.45)

  • Partial Pressure of Oxygen (PaO2): 60 mmHg (80-100)

  • Partial Pressure of Carbon Dioxide (PaCO2): 60 mmHg (35-45)

  • Bicarbonate (HCO3-): 26 mmol/L (22-26)

He was initiated on supplemental oxygen therapy, nebulized bronchodilators, including short-acting beta-agonists and anticholinergics, and systemic corticosteroids were also prescribed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Regarding the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve, which of the following statement is most accurate for this patient?
options: The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is likely shifted to the left due to his elevated PaCO2
The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is likely shifted to the right due to his acidosis and elevated PaCO2
The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is unaffected by changes in this patient's PaCO2 and pH
The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is shifted to the left due to his chronic hypoxemia
The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve will show a higher affinity for oxygen due to his history of COPD
attributes.explanation:
The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve (ODC) demonstrates a sigmoidal relationship between partial pressure of oxygen and oxygen saturation of haemoglobin. The steep lower part of the ODC demonstrates a release of oxygen from haemoglobin with a small change in PaO2, while the flat upper part of the ODC results in a smaller percentage of haemoglobin saturation despite a large increase in PaO2. This non-linear relationship is due to the structure of haemoglobin, which is made up of a heterotetramer composed of four submits that bind to oxygen independently. When one subunit is bound to an oxygen molecule, this results in a conformational change which increases the oxygen affinity of the remaining subunit, changing the haemoglobin from a tensed (deoxygenated) to a relaxed (oxygenated) state. However, the affinity of haemoglobin for oxygen can be impacted by a number of factors, the three most important factors to consider in this scenario are the partial pressure of CO2, pH level and 2,3-DPG.

  • The partial pressure of CO2

    • Increase partial pressure of CO2 = shifts the curve to the right

    • Decrease partial pressure of CO2 = shifts the curve the left

  • pH independent of CO2

    • Decrease pH = shifts the curve to the right

    • Increase pH = shifts the curve to the left

  • The concentration of 2,3-DPG inside erythrocytes

    • Increased 2,3-DPG = shifts the curve to the right

    • Decreased 2,3-DPG = shifts the curve to the left

    • Note: 2,3 DPG is made in response to anaemia or hypoxia

Note that a shift to the left indicates increased hemoglobin affinity for oxygen and an increased reluctance to release oxygen. Conversely, a shift to the right indicates a lower affinity for oxygen.

"The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is likely shifted to the right due to his acidosis and elevated PaCO2" is the most correct answer. Both acidosis (low pH) and elevated PaCO2 (hypercapnia) are factors that shift the dissociation curve to the right. This rightward shift decreases haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen, which helps release oxygen to tissue more readily.

"The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is likely shifted to the left due to his elevated PaCO2" is incorrect. Elevated PaCO2 generally shifts the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve to the right, not the left.

"The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is unaffected by changes in this patient's PaCO2 and pH" is incorrect. The patient's arterial blood gas demonstrates both acidosis and elevated PaCO2 which would significantly influence the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve.

"The oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve is shifted to the left due to his chronic hypoxemia" and "the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve will show a higher affinity for oxygen due to his history of COPD" are both incorrect. Chronic hypoxemia in COPD is associated with a rightward shift in the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve indicating a lower affinity for oxygen. This is because COPD is associated with chronic hypoxia, resulting in elevated 2,3-DPG. In an acute setting, the predominant factors (acidosis and hypercapnia) would also cause a rightward shift rather than a leftward shift.
learningPoints: Acidosis (decreased pH), increased carbon dioxide levels and increased levels of 2,3-DPG causes a rightward shift of the oxyhaemoglobin dissociation curve.
references: Guyton AC, Hall JE. ‘Textbook of Medical Physiology: Eleventh Edition’, Philadelphia, PA, Saunders/Elsevier, 2006.
Kaufman DP, Kandle PF, Murray IV, et al. Physiology, Oxyhemoglobin Dissociation Curve. [Updated 2023 Jul 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan.
